title: Core Verbs & Modal Auxiliaries
situation: Foundation: willen, kunnen, moeten, mogen, gaan, komen, zijn, hebben

phrases.hindi: willen: ik wil, jij wilt/wil, hij wil, wij willen — Ik wil Nederlands leren. Wil jij koffie?
phrases.english: to want: I want, you want, he wants, we want — I want to learn Dutch. Do you want coffee?
phrases.context: Modal + infinitive: the infinitive goes to the END of the clause. 'Ik wil Nederlands leren' — 'leren' is last. 'Willen' = strong want/wish. Informal: 'ik wil' and 'jij wil' (dropping -t) is common.
phrases.pronunciation: WIL-len: ik WIL, yay WILT, hay WIL, way WIL-len — ik WIL NAY-der-lants LAY-ren.

phrases.hindi: kunnen: ik kan, jij kunt/kan, hij kan, wij kunnen — Ik kan Nederlands spreken. Kun jij fietsen?
phrases.english: to be able to/can: I can, you can, he can, we can — I can speak Dutch. Can you cycle?
phrases.context: 'Kunnen' = ability. 'Kan jij...' is one of the most useful question patterns. Note: 'kunt' and 'kan' are both used for 'jij' — 'kan jij?' is very common in speech.
phrases.pronunciation: KUN-nen: ik KAN, yay KUNT, hay KAN, way KUN-nen — ik KAN NAY-der-lants SPRAY-ken.

phrases.hindi: moeten: ik moet, jij moet, hij moet, wij moeten — Ik moet werken. Moet jij morgen vroeg op?
phrases.english: must/have to: I must, you must, he must, we must — I have to work. Do you have to get up early tomorrow?
phrases.context: 'Moeten' = obligation/necessity. Stronger than 'willen'. 'Ik moet weg' = I have to go (common exit phrase). 'Moet je...' = do you need to...?
phrases.pronunciation: MOO-ten: ik MOOT, yay MOOT, hay MOOT, way MOO-ten — ik MOOT WER-ken.

phrases.hindi: mogen: ik mag, jij mag, hij mag, wij mogen — Mag ik binnenkomen? Hier mag je niet roken.
phrases.english: to be allowed to/may: I may, you may, he may, we may — May I come in? You may not smoke here.
phrases.context: 'Mogen' = permission. 'Mag ik...?' = May I...? — very polite question pattern. 'Niet mogen' = not allowed. Essential for asking permission in shops, offices, etc.
phrases.pronunciation: MOH-chen: ik MACH, yay MACH, hay MACH, way MOH-chen — MACH ik BIN-nen-KOH-men?

phrases.hindi: gaan + infinitive (future): Ik ga morgen werken. Wij gaan vanavond eten. Ga jij ook mee?
phrases.english: going to (future): I'm going to work tomorrow. We're going to eat tonight. Are you coming along?
phrases.context: 'Gaan + infinitive' = the most natural way to express future in spoken Dutch. More common than the formal future ('zullen'). 'Vanavond' = this evening. 'Mee' = along/with.
phrases.pronunciation: CHAAN: ik CHAA MOR-gen WER-ken. way CHAAN van-AA-vont AY-ten. CHAA yay OHK MAY?

phrases.hindi: Ik wil niet komen. Ik kan het niet doen. Je moet dat niet vergeten. Dat mag niet.
phrases.english: I don't want to come. I can't do it. You must not forget that. That is not allowed.
phrases.context: Negation with modals: 'niet' goes before the infinitive at the end. 'Ik wil niet komen' — 'niet' + 'komen'. 'Dat mag niet' — when there's no infinitive, 'niet' goes at end.
phrases.pronunciation: ik WIL NEET KOH-men. ik KAN het NEET DOON. ye MOOT dat NEET ver-CHE-ten. dat MACH NEET.

phrases.hindi: Zou jij kunnen helpen? Zou ik een vraag mogen stellen? Ik zou graag koffie willen.
phrases.english: Could you help? May I ask a question? I would like coffee.
phrases.context: 'Zouden' = would/could — the conditional/polite form. 'Zou jij kunnen...?' = could you...? 'Ik zou graag... willen' = I would like... — the polite way to make requests in formal situations.
phrases.pronunciation: ZOW yay KUN-nen HEL-pen? ZOW ik uhn FRAACH MOH-chen STEL-len? ik ZOW CHAACH KO-fee WIL-len.

grammar_notes: Modal verb structure: modal (position 2) + [rest of sentence] + infinitive (at end). 'Ik moet [morgen vroeg] opstaan' — 'opstaan' is last, and separable verbs rejoin in this position
Double infinitive with perfect tense: 'Ik heb niet kunnen komen' (I was not able to come). The order is: heb + [niet] + kunnen + komen. The modal becomes an infinitive after heb.
Modal conjugations are often the same across persons: 'moeten': ik/jij/hij must all say 'moet'. Only 'willen' and 'kunnen' vary
Common fixed expressions: 'ik moet gaan' (I have to go), 'mag ik?' (may I?), 'kan ik helpen?' (can I help?), 'wil je thee?' (do you want tea?)
culture_notes: Dutch directness: Dutch people often say 'ik wil...' where English speakers would say 'could I possibly...' — it's not rude, just direct
'Zouden' + modal makes requests very polite — useful in formal settings like government offices, banks, or when writing emails
Cycling culture: 'fietsen' (to cycle) is so ingrained that 'kun jij fietsen?' is a genuine question people ask about life skills

skill_breakdown.explanation: Dutch modals are irregular but follow patterns. Most are the same across ik/jij/hij. Key conjugations: willen (wil/wilt/wil/willen), kunnen (kan/kunt/kan/kunnen), moeten (moet/moet/moet/moeten), mogen (mag/mag/mag/mogen), zullen (zal/zal/zal/zullen), zouden (zou/zou/zou/zouden).

more_examples.english: We are allowed to park here.

skill_breakdown.explanation: The most common future construction in spoken Dutch. 'Gaan' conjugates normally (ik ga, jij gaat, etc.), and the infinitive goes to the end. Much more natural than 'zullen' for everyday plans.

more_examples.hindi: Ik ga dit weekend naar Den Haag.
more_examples.english: I'm going to The Hague this weekend.

more_examples.hindi: Gaan jullie ook naar het feest?
more_examples.english: Are you all going to the party too?

more_examples.hindi: Hij gaat een nieuwe baan zoeken.
more_examples.english: He's going to look for a new job.

practice_prompt: Dutch modal verbs — willen, kunnen, moeten, mogen, and gaan for expressing wants, abilities, and future plans

theory.intro: Dutch modals (`kunnen`, `moeten`, `mogen`, `willen`, `zullen`) are some of the highest-frequency verbs you'll use. They follow Dutch's signature structure: the modal conjugates and sits in V2 position, while the main verb infinitive flies to the END of the clause. This chapter walks through each modal's meaning, the conjugation pattern, polite-request constructions with `zou`, and where to place `niet` for negation.

sections.heading: The five core modals
sections.body:
Dutch has five workhorse modal verbs. Each carries a distinct flavor of want, need, possibility, or permission — and each is irregular in its singular forms. Learn them as a set.

English speakers already know this terrain (`can`, `must`, `may`, `want`, `shall`), but Dutch modals behave differently in one key way: they conjugate fully and act like normal verbs. There's no separate "would" / "could" / "should" frozen form — Dutch uses real conjugations like `zou` and `kon` instead.

callout.body: English uses these same verbs but treats `could`, `would`, `should`, `might` as frozen forms. Dutch doesn't — it actually conjugates `zou`, `kon`, `mocht`, `wilde` as real verb forms. That's good news: once you know the present, the past and conditional are just more conjugations of the same verb.

sections.cutting_intro: Hi! Let's meet the five modals. They're irregular but they all behave the same way — and you'll use them in almost every sentence.

quick_check.question: Which modal expresses permission (`may` / `be allowed to`)?
quick_check.explanation: `Mogen` is permission — `Mag ik...?` means May I...? Don't confuse with `kunnen` (ability) or `moeten` (obligation).

sections.heading: Modal + infinitive: structure is fixed
sections.body:
Every modal sentence in Dutch follows the same skeleton: SUBJECT + MODAL (in V2 position) + (other elements) + INFINITIVE (at the very end). The modal conjugates and stays in second position. The main verb stays in its dictionary form (the infinitive) and goes to the END of the clause.

What sits between them? Anything else — objects, time markers, places, adverbs. They all pack into the middle, with the infinitive holding the final spot like a closing bracket.

examples.breakdown: kan = V2 position; spreken = infinitive at end. Object `Nederlands` sits in the middle.
moet = V2; wachten = end. Place adverb `hier` in the middle.

examples.hindi: We willen morgen naar Amsterdam gaan.
examples.english: We want to go to Amsterdam tomorrow.
examples.breakdown: willen = V2; gaan = end. Time `morgen` + place `naar Amsterdam` stack in the middle.

callout.body: English speakers instinctively want to put the infinitive right after the modal: `Ik kan spreken Nederlands.` This is WRONG in Dutch. The infinitive must go to the END. Push it back: `Ik kan Nederlands spreken.` Drill this until it feels automatic — it's the single most common modal mistake.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's the golden rule. The modal stays in V2 — and the main verb runs to the END of the sentence. Like bookends.

quick_check.question: What's the correct word order for `I want to learn Dutch`?
quick_check.explanation: Yes! The modal `wil` stays in V2 (second position), and the infinitive `leren` flies to the end. Object `Nederlands` slots between.

sections.heading: Full conjugations of the three most-used modals
sections.body:
Here are the complete present-tense conjugations for `kunnen`, `moeten`, and `willen` — the three you'll use most. Notice the irregular singular forms and the tricky `jij` form that's spelled `kunt` / `wilt` in statements but often drops to `kun` / `wil` when inverted in questions.

Memorize these tables. Once you have them, the other two modals (`mogen` and `zullen`) follow the same shape: invariable in the singular, plus regular `-en` in the plural.

examples.breakdown: Inverted question — `kunt` drops to `kun`. Note `helpen` at end.

examples.english: Do you want a cup of tea?
examples.breakdown: Inverted `wilt` drops to `wil`. Object `een kopje thee` in middle, no infinitive needed here.

callout.body: In inverted `jij`-questions, `kunnen` becomes `kun` (not `kunt`) and `willen` becomes `wil` (not `wilt`). So `kun jij?` and `wil jij?` — irregular drops of the -t. Native speakers do this automatically. Memorize it.

sections.cutting_intro: Time for the tables. These three are your everyday modals. Notice the irregular singulars and the sneaky drop of -t in inverted questions.

quick_check.question: Which is the correct inverted question form for `Can you cycle?`
quick_check.explanation: `Kun jij?` — the -t drops in inverted `jij` questions. `Kunt jij?` is technically tolerated but `kun jij` is what natives actually say.

sections.heading: Polite requests with `zou`
sections.body:
`Zou je` / `zou u` / `zouden jullie` = `would you`. This is the conditional form of `zullen`, and it's the politest way to ask for anything in Dutch. Combine it with another modal (`kunnen` or `willen`) for maximum politeness — literally "would you be able to..." or "would you want to..."

In Dutch service contexts — restaurants, shops, the gemeente, doctor's offices — `zou je / zou u kunnen` is the gold standard. Direct imperatives (`geef me het zout`) feel rude unless you know the person well. When in doubt, soften with `zou`.

table.caption: Casual vs polite request patterns
rows: Zou je het zout willen geven?

examples.hindi: Zou u mij kunnen vertellen waar het station is?
examples.english: Could you tell me where the station is?
examples.breakdown: `u` = formal you; `zou ... kunnen` = could; `vertellen` infinitive at end.

examples.hindi: Ik zou graag een afspraak willen maken.
examples.english: I would like to make an appointment.
examples.breakdown: `Ik zou graag ... willen` = I would like — the standard polite formula.

examples.english: May I interrupt for a moment?
examples.breakdown: `Mag ik...?` is already polite — no `zou` needed for short permission requests.

callout.body: Memorize one formula: `Ik zou graag ... willen` (I would like ...). Slot any noun in the gap: `ik zou graag koffie willen`, `ik zou graag de rekening willen` (the bill). It's the politest way to order, request, or ask for anything in Dutch.

sections.cutting_intro: Want to sound polite? Use `zou`. Pair it with another modal and you've got the gold-standard Dutch service-counter phrase.

quick_check.question: What's the politest way to ask `Could you help me?` in Dutch?
quick_check.explanation: `Zou je me kunnen helpen?` — `zou` + `kunnen` is the most polite stack. Literally `would you be able to help me`. Use it at the gemeente, bank, or with strangers.

sections.heading: Negation with modals
sections.body:
`Niet` (not) has a specific home in modal sentences: it goes BEFORE the infinitive but AFTER any objects or time/place phrases. The rule of thumb: put `niet` as close to the END of the sentence as possible, but always before the final infinitive.

For indefinite negation — when you'd say `not a` or `no` in English — Dutch uses `geen` instead of `niet`. `Geen` replaces the indefinite article `een`: `Ik wil een koffie` becomes `Ik wil geen koffie` (I don't want any coffee). Never say `niet een` — it's always `geen`.

examples.hindi: Ik kan dit boek niet lezen.
examples.breakdown: Object `dit boek` first, then `niet`, then infinitive `lezen` at end.
No object — `niet` sits directly before the infinitive `gaan`.

examples.hindi: Je mag dat hier niet doen.
examples.english: You're not allowed to do that here.

examples.hindi: We hebben geen tijd om te gaan.
examples.english: We don't have time to go.

callout.body: Placing `niet` in the wrong spot is one of the most common Dutch errors. Default rule: put `niet` as close to the END as possible, but always BEFORE the final infinitive. And remember — for `not a` / `no`, use `geen`, not `niet een`. Never mix them.

sections.cutting_intro: Last thing — negation. `Niet` has a specific home: just before the infinitive at the end. And `geen` replaces `een` for indefinite negation.

quick_check.question: Where does `niet` go in `Ik kan dit boek ___ lezen` (I can't read this book)?
options: After `kan`: Ik kan niet dit boek lezen.
Before `lezen`: Ik kan dit boek niet lezen.
At the start: Niet ik kan dit boek lezen.
quick_check.explanation: Yes! `Niet` goes BEFORE the infinitive but AFTER the object. So: `Ik kan dit boek niet lezen.` Push it as close to the end as possible while still before `lezen`.

theory.wrap_up: Modals are the engine of everyday Dutch — they power polite requests, expressions of necessity, statements of plans, and questions of permission. Master the V2 + infinitive-at-end structure first, then drill the irregular singular forms of the five core modals, then practice `zou` for politeness. The phrases below give you mixed-modal sentences for ordering, asking permission, expressing wants, and making polite requests at the gemeente or restaurant. Pay attention to where the infinitive lands — it should always be the last word.
